analogy: Think of "Indian Bronze Sculpture" through the chapter's own opening images: INDIAN sculptors had mastered the bronze medium and the casting process as much as they had mastered terracotta sculpture and carving in stone. The cire-perdu or ‘lost-wax’ process for casting was learnt as long ago as the Indus Valley Culture. Along with it was discovered... The easy idea is that the visible surface is only the outside cover. The chapter keeps asking how we can know what is inside when no one can directly reach the deep interior. So the learner must read clues: rocks, mines, drilling, volcanic material, heat, gravity, magnetism and earthquake waves. Like solving a hidden-layer puzzle, each clue tells something about the crust, mantle, core and volcanic activity without inventing anything beyond the textbook.
full_notes:
Indian Bronze Sculpture is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins by asking how the nature of the earth should be imagined and why the interior cannot be known in the same direct way as the surface. It connects the hidden interior with visible results on the surface, especially landscape development, earthquakes, tsunamis and volcanic activity.

The text explains that iNDIAN sculptors had mastered the bronze medium and the casting process as much as they had mastered terracotta sculpture and carving in stone.

The text explains that the cire-perdu or ‘lost-wax’ process for casting was learnt as long ago as the Indus Valley Culture.

The text explains that along with it was discovered the process of making alloy of metals by mixing copper, zinc and tin which is called bronze.

The text explains that bronze sculptures and statuettes of Buddhist, Hindu and Jain icons have been discovered from many regions of India dating from the second century until the sixteenth century.

The text explains that most of these were used for ritual worship and are characterised by exquisite beauty and aesthetic appeal.

The text explains that at the same time the metal-casting process continued to be utilised for making articles for various purposes of daily use, such as utensils for cooking, eating, drinking, etc.

The text explains that present-day tribal communities also utilise the ‘lost-wax’ process for their art expressions.

The text explains that perhaps the ‘Dancing Girl’ from Mohenjodaro is the earliest bronze sculpture datable to 2500 BCE.

The text explains that the limbs and torso of this female figurine are simplified in tubular form.

The text explains that a similar group of bronze statuettes have been discovered from archaeological excavation at Daimabad (Maharashtra) datable to 1500 BCE.

The text explains that significant is the ‘Chariot’, the wheels of which are represented in simple circular shapes while the driver or human rider has been elongated, and the bulls in the forefront are modelled in sturdy forms.

The text explains that interesting images of Jain tirthankaras have been discovered from Chausa, Bihar, belonging to the Kushana Period during second century CE.

The text explains that these bronzes show how the Indian sculptors had mastered the modelling of masculine human physique and simplified muscles.

The text explains that remarkable is the depiction of Adinath or Vrishabhnath, who is identified with long hairlocks dropping to his shoulders.

The text explains that otherwise the tirthankaras are noted by their short curly hair.

The text explains that gujarat and Rajasthan have been strongholds of Jainism since early times.

The text explains that although bronze images were modelled and cast during the Pallava Period in the eighth and ninth centuries, some of the most beautiful and exquisite statues were produced during the Chola Period in Tamil Nadu from the tenth to the twelfth century.

The text explains that among the Pallava Period bronzes of the eighth century is the icon of Shiva seated in ardhaparyanka asana (one leg kept dangling).

The text explains that a wide range of Shiva iconography was evolved in the Thanjavur (Tanjore) region of Tamil Nadu.

The text explains that the ninth century kalyanasundara murti is highly remarkable for the manner in which Panigrahana (ceremony of marriage) is represented by two separate statuettes.

The text explains that shiva with his extended right hand accepts Parvati’s (the bride’s) right hand, who is depicted with a bashful expression and taking a step forward.

The text explains that the union of Shiva and Parvati is very ingeniously represented in the ardhanarisvara murti in a single image.

The text explains that beautiful independent figurines of Parvati have also been modelled, standing in graceful tribhanga posture.

The text explains that during the sixteenth century, known as the Vijayanagar Period in Andhra Pradesh, the sculptors experimented with portrait sculpture in order to preserve knowledge of the royal patron for posterity.

The text explains that at Tirupati, life-size standing portrait statues were cast in bronze, depicting Krishnadevaraya with his two queens, Tirumalamba and Chinnadevi.

The text explains that the sculptor has combined the likeness of the facial features with certain elements of idealisation.

The text explains that the idealisation is further observed in the manner the physical body is modelled to appear imposing as well as graceful.

The text explains that the standing king and queens are depicted in praying posture, that is, both hands held in the namaskara mudra.

Source: NCERT Class 11 An Introduction to Indian Art, Chapter 7: Indian Bronze Sculpture

prelims_traps: Do not treat INDIAN BRONZE and SCULPTURE as identical unless the source text links them directly.
Do not add outside examples to Indian Bronze Sculpture; use only the examples and processes named in the chapter.
Do not confuse a visible surface result with the internal process or source evidence described by the text.
mains_angles: Explain Indian Bronze Sculpture through the source chain: evidence, process and visible result.
Use INDIAN BRONZE and SCULPTURE to show how the chapter connects facts with mechanisms.
Frame an answer around causes, internal working, surface expression and source limitations from the NCERT chapter.
